core_concept: Cold War, Decolonization, and Late 20th-Century World
facts: The Cold War was a rivalry between the United States and the Soviet Union, not a direct military conflict between them, because both superpowers avoided open war due to the threat of nuclear annihilation.
Decolonization after World War II freed many Asian and African countries; India became independent in 1947 through nonviolent resistance led by Gandhi, not through a war of liberation.
The Chinese Revolution of 1949 brought Mao Zedong and the Communist Party to power, ending a long civil war, which shifted global power as China aligned with the Soviet Union.
Proxy wars such as the Korean War (1950–1953) and the Vietnam War (1955–1975) allowed the US and USSR to compete through local forces without fighting each other directly.
The Berlin Wall was erected in 1961 to stop East Germans from fleeing to West Berlin, and its fall in 1989 symbolized the end of Cold War divisions in Europe.
The Soviet Union collapsed in 1991 primarily because of economic stagnation and political reforms under Gorbachev, not because of a military defeat by the United States.
African independence movements in the 1950s and 1960s created new nations like Ghana (1957) as European powers weakened after World War II and could no longer maintain colonies.
misconceptions: Students think the Cold War involved direct battles between US and Soviet troops, but the superpowers never fought each other directly; they waged proxy wars instead.
Students believe India gained independence simply because Britain left after World War II, ignoring the central role of Gandhi's nonviolent movement in forcing British withdrawal.
Students think the Soviet Union collapsed because it lost a war in Afghanistan, when in fact economic problems and internal reforms, not military defeat, brought down the USSR.
